They sink to the bottom and stay there, preserved indefinitely.

members.name: Nox

Nox serves as the reluctant interface between Zelidia and the outside world—a shadow-market where non-pixies can trade under heavy restriction. Transactions occur at night (hence the name), conducted through intermediaries, with the actual Zelle traders remaining unseen in the darkness above. Weapons are forbidden. Lights are forbidden. Those who break the rules simply vanish.

Logging camps work the northern reaches, sending timber down to Zamrat Aqabah's shipyards. The deeper portions of the Whitewood remain wild and largely unexplored. members.name: Whitewood

The trees here are pale as bone, their leaves producing a distinctive rustling that the elves interpret as ancestral voices. Logging is forbidden; the forest is maintained as a shrine to the old ways. Taoinor's faction draws spiritual legitimacy from their control of Whitewood.

The pass climbs to approximately 11,000 feet at its highest point, threading between peaks that rise several thousand feet higher on either side. members.name: The Evacuation
members.contentPreview: Mosimor was evacuated along with Ot'Pelyon. The processing facilities were abandoned mid-operation. Materials from Ot'Pelyon—possibly the most contaminated in the entire southern region—sat in the workshops when everyone left.

members.name: The Evacuation
members.contentPreview: Myrth was evacuated along with Novranas and Salenial. The processing equipment was left behind. Partially processed materials sat in the workshops, never completed. The priests and workers were relocated to quarantine settlements. Their specialized knowledge died with them over the following decades.

members.name: The Evacuation
members.contentPreview: The evacuation was orderly. The priesthood gave everyone time to pack essential belongings. They were relocated to quarantine settlements in the high mountains. Most of the evacuees died within a generation. The contamination had already taken hold. Novranas was left behind—buildings intact, streets empty, records still filed in the administrative halls.

members.name: The Evacuation
members.contentPreview: Ot'Pelyon was evacuated early in the crisis—possibly before anyone fully understood what was happening. The population was relocated to quarantine settlements. Most died quickly; their exposure had been severe.

members.name: The Evacuation
members.contentPreview: Salenial was evacuated along with Novranas and Myrth. The miners and their families were relocated to quarantine settlements. Most didn't survive long—their daily contact with the contaminated materials had exposed them heavily. The village was left behind. The quarries were abandoned mid-operation. Equipment sits where it was dropped.

members.contentPreview: Ruins within Hilda's Forest, marked on maps but rarely visited. The structures predate elven settlement—their architecture matches nothing else in the region. Probably.

members.name: The Contamination
members.contentPreview: Carya Nairn was evacuated along with the other southern sites. The region now sits within the larger quarantine zone, accessible only to authorized priesthood observation teams—and they don't stay long.

members.name: The Contamination
members.contentPreview: The southern harvesting disaster struck the Eshbya Hills hardest. The villages of Novranas, Salenial, and Myrth all had to be evacuated when workers began showing symptoms: wasting sickness, skin discoloration, children born wrong. The specific cause remains sealed in priesthood archives. What's known publicly is that certain titan remains in this region were contaminated—not with anything so simple as disease, but with something that spread through contact and persisted across generations.

members.name: The Contamination
members.contentPreview: Kirthdron Soytas was evacuated along with the other southern sites 200 years ago. The region may have been the last to show contamination symptoms, or it may simply have had a smaller population to evacuate. Either way, it's been closed ever since.

members.name: The Contamination
members.contentPreview: Like the other southern sites, Tomgryir Eror was evacuated when workers began showing symptoms. The villages of Ot'Pelyon and Mosimor were both abandoned within months of each other. The contamination in Tomgryir Eror may have been the original source—some priestly records suggest the first cases appeared here before spreading to neighboring regions. If true, this area may be more dangerous than the others.

Fights in the Labyrinth are strange. The maze reconfigures between bouts. Shadows interfere unpredictably. The arena floor is sprinkled with gold dust before each match, turning the sand glittering and slick. members.name: Dragon Relations
members.contentPreview: The East Naruaghin occupy a narrow corridor between the territories of Pythalomos and Senwyn. Neither dragon hunts here regularly—they prefer the deeper jungle where prey can't escape. This relative safety from dragon predation is one reason the eastern tribes have grown strong enough to resist the Dwelyn. The tribes maintain a wary respect for Surrey Mahaila, despite the war. The emerald dragon is ancient, and Naruaghin oral tradition speaks of her presence long before the Dwelyn arrived. Some

members.name: Dragon Relations
members.contentPreview: Pythalomos, the constrictor dragon, terrorizes the North Naruaghin more than any other tribe. The dragon's territory overlaps significantly with their lands, and it treats their villages as hunting grounds. The North Naruaghin don't fight Pythalomos—they hide, scatter, and rebuild. They hate the dragon with cold, patient loathing, and their bone temples include effigies of the serpent that they ritually desecrate.

members.name: Dragon Relations
members.contentPreview: Senwyn the Agonizer treats the South Naruaghin as prey and entertainment. The dragon doesn't just hunt them—it toys with them, poisoning water sources and watching villages die slowly, or striking hunting parties and leaving survivors to spread terror. The South Naruaghin hate Senwyn with burning intensity, and their shamans work constantly on poisons they hope might one day harm the dragon.

members.name: Relations with Other Tribes
members.contentPreview: The other Naruaghin view the East with complicated feelings. The South raid them for resources and warriors; the North consider them corrupted by foreign contact. Yet when major threats emerge—dragon attacks, demon incursions—the East can call on tribal solidarity. Blood is blood. The East Naruaghin increasingly see themselves as the Naruaghin future: organized, disciplined, capable of facing external threats. They pity their cousins, trapped fighting dragons and demons while the East builds som

members.name: Relations with Other Tribes
members.contentPreview: The North Naruaghin are the most isolationist of their people. They rarely participate in the intertribal wars that occupy the South and East, preferring to husband their strength against the greater threat from below. Other Naruaghin consider them cowards. The North Naruaghin consider everyone else fools who don't understand what's coming.

members.name: Relations with Other Tribes
members.contentPreview: The South Naruaghin are constantly raiding. They push against the East Naruaghin, compete with them for hunting grounds, and occasionally launch expeditions against the North (though the northern tribes' territory offers little worth taking). They view themselves as the true Naruaghin—the others have grown soft.

members.name: Husakas
members.contentPreview: A desert region in the southwest Dunes of Kunagi, roamed by scorpion riders who have adapted to the harsh conditions. The water here carries a terrible curse—drinking causes complete loss of long-term memory. This has shaped the Husakas people's entire culture around memory preservation and scorpion symbiosis. See the full [Husakas](Husakas.md) entry for the scorpion rider culture, the Twice-Born, and notable locations.

Husakas is both a place and a people—the scorpion riders who have claimed this unforgiving territory as home for longer than anyone can remember. Which is fitting, given what the land does to memory.

See the full [House of the Scorpion](House%20of%20the%20Scorpion.md) entry for the Prince, the pilgrimage ritual, and why killing him would be catastrophic.

members.name: Gyerekas
members.contentPreview: The fossil reef hills marking Husakas's southern boundary—ancient coral formations and the bones of creatures from a vanished sea. The Husakas call it the Bone Garden. It serves as neutral trading ground and contains dangerous cave systems leading into unknown depths. See the full [Gyerekas](Gyerekas.md) entry for the reef's resources, the Leviathan's Rest, and the Dwellers Below.

members.name: Gyerekas
members.contentPreview: The southern boundary of the Dunes of Kunagi, where the sand sea breaks against a range of low, strange hills that aren't hills at all. Gyerekas is a fossilized reef—the remains of an ocean floor from an age when the desert was seafloor, now thrust up and exposed by geological forces older than human memory. The Husakas call it the Bone Garden. They're not far wrong.

A patchwork of verdant islands fed by underground springs, home to the Spotted Ones (mixed Husakas-Chule descent) who serve as intermediaries between desert and grassland cultures. See the full [Spotted Forest](Spotted%20Forest.md) entry for the oasis ecology, trading crossroads, and current tensions.

members.name: Geography and Position
members.contentPreview: Balduahr occupies a section of the mountains where the Kilbyurns descend toward the plains. The kingdom controls three defensible valleys connected by tunnels and fortified passes. Beyond these valleys, the mountains become Kilbyurn territory proper—and the dwarves don't go there. The Shivyll River originates in Balduahr's territory, providing water, power, and a transportation route. The river also provides the kingdom's greatest vulnerability: it flows east, directly into satyr lands.
